title: Bilingual Child Language Exposure Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Many Hours for Bilingual Kids?
description: Find out how many hours your child needs for active bilingualism. Free calculator based on Pearson & De Houwer research.
intro: A child can develop simultaneous bilingualism if exposed to significant input in both languages during the first 6-7 years. Research (Pearson 2008; De Houwer 2007) shows that at least 30% of daily input must come from the minority language for active language development. This calculator helps you determine if your family's plan—hours with each parent, school, grandparents, media—reaches that threshold and alerts you if your child might remain passive bilingual (understands but doesn't actively speak). Perfect for mixed families, immigrant households, or parents wanting their children to maintain a heritage language or learn English from an early age.
keyTakeaway: For **active** bilingualism, your child needs at least **30% daily input** in the minority language (~25 hours/week). Less produces **passive** bilingualism (understands but doesn't speak).
useCases: Argentine parent in Canada who wants their child to speak Spanish.
Bilingual couple using OPOL (One Parent, One Language) strategy.
Bilingual school enrollment—is it enough exposure?
English-speaking nanny for a few hours per week.

fields.label: Hours/week of school or activity in minority language
Hours/week of media (TV, books, music) in minority language

example.title: Example: 15h parent + 0 school + 5 media, age 2
example.result: 24% — below the threshold. Risk of passive bilingualism.

explanation:
## How It Works

Children under 6-7 years develop two native languages without an accent if exposed to sufficient input:

| % Minority Language | Outcome |
|---|---|
| <15% | Weak receptive skills |
| 15-30% | Passive bilingual |
| **30-50%** | Active bilingual |
| >50% | Dominant minority language |

## Strategies

1. **OPOL (One Parent, One Language)**: Each parent speaks only one language to the child.
2. **mL@H (Minority Language at Home)**: Minority language at home, majority language at school.
3. **Time & Place**: Designate specific days, times, or locations for each language.
4. **External reinforcement**: Grandparents, nannies, playgroups, or cultural groups speaking the minority language.

## The Critical Window

Simultaneous bilingualism closes around age 7. Between 7-12, children can still develop strong skills with a slight accent. After 12, a new language is learned as a second language (L2), using different neural pathways.

## Potential Risks

- **Inconsistency** → weak bilingual development.
- **Strong majority language pressure** (>80% input) → language loss in minority language.
- **Passive media without interaction** doesn't count—live human interaction is key.

Also check out our [parallel language learning calculator](/calculadora-idioma-paralelo-2-al-mismo) for adults.

faq.q: Can kids learn two languages at the same time without getting confused?
faq.a: No, bilingual children don't get confused. They may mix words between 2-3 years (code-switching, which is normal), but they naturally separate the languages by 3-4 years.

faq.q: How many hours per week does my child need to become bilingual?
faq.a: Research shows at least 30% of daily input should be in the minority language. For a child awake ~84 hours/week, that's roughly 25+ hours/week in the minority language for active bilingualism.

faq.q: What's the difference between active and passive bilingualism?
faq.a: Active bilingualism: your child understands and speaks both languages. Passive bilingualism: they understand but don't actively produce speech in one language. Active requires more exposure (30%+); passive results from less input (15-30%).

faq.q: Does watching TV or YouTube in another language count?
faq.a: Partially. Screens provide receptive input but limited active production. One-on-one interaction with a live speaker is far more effective. Combine screen time with conversation for better results.

faq.q: How old should my child be to start learning a second language?
faq.a: The best time is from birth (simultaneous bilingualism). If starting later, up to age 6-7 still allows near-native acquisition. After age 7, it becomes sequential bilingualism (L2), which takes longer but is still very achievable.

faq.q: Does bilingual school provide enough exposure?
faq.a: If a bilingual school offers 20-25 hours/week in the minority language plus family support at home, yes, it can work. But school alone without home reinforcement may not be enough for active production.

faq.q: What is the OPOL method (One Parent, One Language)?
faq.a: OPOL is a popular strategy where one parent speaks only the minority language to the child, and the other parent speaks only the majority language. It's simple, consistent, and scientifically supported for building balanced bilingualism.

The calculator is based on peer-reviewed research (Pearson 2008, De Houwer 2007). Results are indicative and serve as a reference. For major life decisions involving language education or child development, consult a speech pathologist or bilingualism specialist.

faq.q: Is this calculator free? Do I need to sign up?
faq.a: Yes, 100% free. We don't ask for email, registration, or personal data. Use it as many times as you like.
